---
title: "btrfs restores busted saucy desktops"
date: 2013-05-18
forum: Ubuntu Development Version
---

### Post by ventrical on 2013-05-18
I just wanted to start a new  thread on btrfs file system format.  I have been experimenting with it off and on and  had done an upgrade to saucy from raring back a few weeks ago and used btrfs as the file format. A week and a half ago I installed apt-btrfs-snapshot and ran an undate/upgrade (several times) giving me a variety of snapshots to choose from. 

Yesterday I updated and it busted my desktop. Lightdm and logon came up, but, after logon I got no desktop. Just mouse pointer. (Most likely a compiz problem). Rather than start removing and reconfiguring things I decided to  'rollback' and here I am typing from the rolled back snapshot ! :)

 Since the devs put "mir' to sleep for the desktop, I sort of got  bored and so I am going to experiment with the other snapshots, meaning to say is that I am going to format a saucy system with btrfs and then try to insert  the @snapshot onto that machine.  Finally .. it's fun again :)

regards,
ventrical

---

### Post by ventrical on 2013-05-18
Just ran into a big problem. It appears that this procedure will not save the ccsm settings and/or knocks out Unity launcher - so I have a desktop with no panels but, oddly enough, the rotating cube works just great. I have 5 more snapshots to try .. but there was only one instance where I lost the desktop - not in the saved snapshots.

Edit:

 Looks like the snapshots are not making it to grub. There seems to be a real bug with saucy - or something I am doing wrong. The current partition I am using is sda12 where the btrfs is mounted but the info suggests to use /sda1/ where I keep getting a file not found error of some sort.

It worked just fine on another hdd with raring using /sda1/ so I am not totally sure if btrfs is /sda1/ specific only atm ..

---

### Post by ventrical on 2013-05-18
Cannot copy a btrfs file to an ext4 filesystem because it does not support symlinks so I am going to format another hdd and use my USB bridge to copy a snapshot from a btrfs system to a btrfs system. Theoretically I should be able to 'rollup' as well as 'rollback', meaning to say that if I install raring or quantal with btrfs then I should be able to /insert/ that saucy snapshot because apt-btrfs-snapshot will actually think it is rolling back!:) This then would be a virtual model of a rolling release - roll ahead to the bleeding edge with the saftey of rolling back to a more stable plaform , even nostalgic snapshots of earlier Ubuntu.

---

### Post by ventrical on 2013-05-20
Synopsis:

Currently I was able to rollback to an earlier date within a Saucy install. I assume there is a bug (with saucy) because it seems to loose the Unity or Compiz config settings. I am able to load gnome-session fallback, create a new user and then Unity panel is back to normal in new user.

  In raring , this did not pose a problem.

Secondly, the snapshots, (according to a lot of reading) are not  in a format where one snapshot can be rolled forward on the back of an ealier release.  This of course presents a problem. However, I think there is a way to slipstream and snapshot in using  a little experiementation.

---

### Post by ventrical on 2013-05-20
This is not a problem with saucy , but, more a problem with the way I installed  Saucy on a  partition. The rules go somewhat like this:

**1 Preliminary Note**

 In this tutorial I have installed the whole system on a btrfs file system, i.e., there's no separate /boot partition on an ext file system. If you use a separate /boot partition and apt installs anything in that partition (like a new kernel), you cannot undo changes to the /boot partition with apt-btrfs-snapshot- only changes on the btrfs partition can be reverted. 

and this is from - 

[http://www.howtoforge.com/rollback-to-a-working-state-with-btrfs-plus-apt-btrfs-snapshot-on-ubuntu-12.10](http://www.howtoforge.com/rollback-to-a-working-state-with-btrfs-plus-apt-btrfs-snapshot-on-ubuntu-12.10)

Now I am not 100% sure if this is the case but I am experimenting further with that assumption in mind. My next logical step would be to do a clean install of Saucy on a  single drive with btrfs only excluding any other partition.

---

### Post by grahammechanical on 2013-05-20
> [COLOR=#000000]In this tutorial I have installed the whole system on a btrfs file system, i.e., there's no separate /boot partition on an ext file system. If you use a separate /boot partition and apt installs anything in that partition (like a new kernel), you cannot undo changes to the /boot partition with apt-btrfs-snapshot- only changes on the btrfs partition can be reverted. [/COLOR]

That would make logical sense, Mr Spock. What I am wondering is, does Grub read a btfs file system? When I tried installing Ubuntu with btrfs I got an "Executing 'grub-install' /dev/asda failed" error. 

I also tried (this with Quantal) installing on Ext4 and then converting to btrfs. Update-grub (on another Ubuntu) detected the new installation but booting to it from Grub gave  "error no such device uuid blah, blah" with a dump at an intramfs prompt.

All of which caused me to wonder if Grub can read a btrfs file system. It is illogical to think that installing Ubuntu + btrfs as the only OS on a hard disk would succeed where having more than one OS does not succeed. <raised eyebrow>

Has anyone worked out there has recently been a rerun on UK TV of the Star trek movies?

Live long and prosper.

### Post by ventrical on 2013-05-20

  I had a working system in raring (if you look at other thread) with Grub. It was originally ext4. I had Quantal on it and then did a "install alongside' of raring (btrfs) and it worked fine. I was even able to roll back from raring to quantal but I really had to fanagle with the configuration - meaning that I had to remove apt-btrfs-snapshot at a certain point and then reinstall it. However, I have been reading other subjects on the matter and I noted that some threads indicate that GruB and BTrfs are not a good idea.

Then , roll back , and after doing my proccedure of removing and reinstalling apt-btrfs-snapshot, take a snapshot of the Saucy install.. then attempt to rollback to the raring install and then , from there , attempt to rollup back to saucy !

Ok... big boo boo here. My bad. I forgot to disable  apt-btrfs-snapshot before I updated to the proprietary Nvidia driver , which , of course, called apt and hence engaged apt-btrfs-snapshot in the background. Hmm interesting ... but why three extra snapshots ??


ok.. time to upgrade to Saucy - disable apt-btrfs-snapshot

### Post by ventrical on 2013-05-20
While the upgrade is going on I just wanted to jot down some notes here .. the idea being that theoretically a btrfs snapshot can be downloaded and 'rolled-up' on the an earlier development cycle almost like a daily .iso file. It would only be exclusive to btrfs but would be a true model of a 'rolling release'.

### Post by ventrical on 2013-05-20
I am totally stunned! I seamlessly rolled back to fresh install of raring.

Ok.. now to 'rollup" back to saucy. First I have to reinstall apt-btrfs-snapshot in the raring install.??? Hmmm apt-btrfs-snapshot is already installed. I'm amazed still again.

```

root@ventrical-desktop:~# apt-btrfs-snapshot list

```

root@ventrical-desktop:~# apt-btrfs-snapshot list
Available snapshots:

### Post by ventrical on 2013-05-20
Seamless development cycle ping-pong!:)

```

ventrical@ventrical-desktop:~$ lsb_release -a
No LSB modules are available.
Distributor ID:    Ubuntu
Description:    Ubuntu Saucy Salamander (development branch)
Release:    13.10
Codename:    saucy
ventrical@ventrical-desktop:~$ 


```

therefore , hypothetically and also, snapshots can be used from various distribution cycles eliminating the need for added or extended partions.

 I am just baffled that this worked. Kudos to the developers of this little program.

Please note that I did not use an ext4 partion addon on this drive. It was pure root btrfs.


 This would mean that I could 'rollup' a btrfs snapshot if it was created on a similar system - or across mutiple like systems it could be netcast almost like acronis or Norton Ghost.

How sweet it is.

Regards,
Ventrical

### Post by grahammechanical on 2013-05-21
@ventrical

I am following your threads but following does not mean understanding. A question came to me over the weekend. We are agreed that an Ext4 boot partition would not be included in the btrfs snapshot. Which means we will not be able to roll back to an earlier (working kernel). So, my question, imagine 3 btrfs partitions - boot, root (/) & home. Would one snapshot cover all 3 partitions? Or would three separate snapshots be needed? 

I suppose it depends on what we want to safeguard, the home folder or the system. What if the home folder was safe and it was just the OS that was broken? I do not much like the idea of doing a roll back that included the /home partition and then found that my documents were at an earlier stage of work.

Supposing we need a non-btrfs boot partition how much of a problem would that be? All the previous kernels would be there. We would need to match the snapshots with the kernels and then after rolling back boot into the matching kernel. I am thinking that this might be a solution.

So, you have confirmed that 1 x hard disk + 1 x Ubuntu + all btrfs = successful installation? 

Book him Dan-o!

---

### Post by ventrical on 2013-05-21
@grahammechanical

> 


 So, my question, imagine 3 btrfs partitions - boot, root (/) &  home. Would one snapshot cover all 3 partitions? Or would three separate  snapshots be needed? 



Your question perplexes me.  From my minimal experience with apt-btrfs-snapshot I would tend to assume that each partiton could facilitate it's own working snapshot. I would also assume that only one snapshot could be migrated  to all three partitons.

  On this particular install I have only one partiton on a 40GB hdd and that is a btrfs partition. To my bewliderment I did not need an Ext4 partition to be able to perform this rollback - rollup proceedure.

---


Allow me explain one point here. Since I am trying to streamline this proceedure I installed small programs like 'htop' to create a snapshot of the given install or 'synaptic' as with which I installed and created snapshot while in raring . Remember ... we have to use 'apt' to create a snapshot while apt-btrfs-snapshot is installed.e.g. after the fresh install of raring on a btrfs partiton  *I then installed 'synaptic'   using : sudo apt-get install synaptic in terminal. ( I did not want to do a big upgrade of raring). After the snapshot was created I then removed apt-btrfs-snapshot (but I took a shortcut and installed nividia driver first before I removed apt-btrfs-snapshot and that explains the 3 extra snapshots of raring - calling apt using a script in software sources in the background)*. Next I  then changed the sources.list and  then upgraded to Saucy Salamander. After reboot I am in Saucy Salamander with the added caveat that all my firefox tabs are still there (and whatever else migrated with it). THEN I reinstalled apt-btrfs-snapshot and then used apt to create a snapshot of the Saucy Salamander Install- My code was:

sudo apt-get install htop

I did not want to do a big update of Saucy so I just chose a little file which of course would need 'apt' and call apt-btrfs-snapshot.

Now .. the phenomenon is that there is some sort of global back-threading going on because when I rolled back to the Raring install using the very first snapshot on the list, I had found that apt-btrfs-snapshot was still installed!! (which was removed before upgrade to saucy.)

  I thought to myself .... 'what kind of image rollback is this?' and so presumed that the snapshot process is defintely dragging some system folders from @Home or elsewhere.  Of course this is all still experimental. The thing is that GRUB , while running in the background during the upgrade process to saucy recognized and reported the raring kernels (yes there were two Raring [3.8.x.x] and how they got there is beyond me because I do not recall updating) . GRuB will not appear during boot process while holding down shift key. It comes up with verbose statement of Grub Loading... but goes right to  'searching for btrfs filesystem ... etc..

> 
I suppose it depends on what we want to safeguard, the home folder or  the system. What if the home folder was safe and it was just the OS that  was broken? I do not much like the idea of doing a roll back that  included the /home partition and then found that my documents were at an  earlier stage of work.



That is an excellent question but I must admit that I did not do this experiment to cover that particular aspect point. I am generally trying  to prove that I can roll back from one release and roll up to another .  I am certain that btrfs was not designed for the experiment that I carried out, but it did work. I was also able to successfully roll back to an original install (in the other thread) while having an ext4 partition on the same disk (but here again I did not take the time to notice if there were documents in earlier stages of development.


> 

Supposing we need a non-btrfs boot partition how much of a problem would  that be? All the previous kernels would be there. We would need to  match the snapshots with the kernels and then after rolling back boot  into the matching kernel. I am thinking that this might be a solution.



I guess I loosely assumed that the apt-btrfs-snapshot tool would auto-bind with the appropiate kernels , that process being embedded within it's algorithm.

Regards,
Ventrical

### Post by grahammechanical on 2013-05-21
I was basing this

> [COLOR=#000000]Supposing we need a non-btrfs boot partition how much of a problem would that be? All the previous kernels would be there. We would need to match the snapshots with the kernels and then after rolling back boot into the matching kernel. I am thinking that this might be a solution.[/COLOR]


On this in preliminary notes that you quoted from that tutorial.

> [COLOR=#000000]If you use a separate /boot partition and apt installs anything in that partition (like a new kernel), you cannot undo changes to the /boot partition with apt-btrfs-snapshot- only changes on the btrfs partition can be reverted.[/COLOR]

Which seems to say that a roll back to before a system update would not remove a kernel that came in with that update. Now the new kernel might boot the pre-update system just fine but the previous kernel should definitely not have any problems. It is just the way my thoughts were working.

Regards.

---

### Post by ventrical on 2013-05-21
> **grahammechanical said:**
> I was basing this



On this in preliminary notes that you quoted from that tutorial.



Which seems to say that a roll back to before a system update would not remove a kernel that came in with that update. Now the new kernel might boot the pre-update system just fine but the previous kernel should definitely not have any problems. It is just the way my thoughts were working.

Regards.

Yes, precisely. I didn't see it but that is what is happening. The big 'however' on all this is when an ext4 (or other -haven't tried other yet) is  the previous format and then along comes a side by side install with a btrfs partition.  This is where things seem to begin to split hairs. On another one of my installs on a totally different machine the rollbacks all had the Unity panel removed.  Everything else worked (even current ccsm) but I had to install gnome-session-fallback, then, create a new user and give them admin privlidge (could not do it using <settings> as it would not recognize password) and alas, there was Unity. This bug appears to be  only in saucy and not raring and that  *other* install was a fresh install from the Saucy daily-build using btrfs.

  This process is so intricate that it is hard to document comprehensively but I have read  some of the other links and manuals about this  and it appears that many have gone before us in working with this file format - hence it is being hotly developed and the result of this is that it seems very versatile, but those are only my observations from what I have gathered.

Regards..

### Post by ventrical on 2013-05-21
Moving along.. I noticed the option 'set-default' which reverts to a previous snapshot and I am about to try this on  one of my installs that was originally install with ext4 but also has a btrfs install of Saucy on it.

```

ventrical@ventrical-desktop:~$ apt-btrfs-snapshot -h
usage: apt-btrfs-snapshot [-h] [--debug]
                          
                          {supported,list,snapshot,set-default,delete,list-older-than,delete-older-than}
                          ...

Filesystem snapshot support for apt

optional arguments:
  -h, --help            show this help message and exit
  --debug               enable debug output

Commands:
  {supported,list,snapshot,set-default,delete,list-older-than,delete-older-than}
    supported           Print if snapshots are supported
    list                List available snapshots
    snapshot            Create a new snapshot
    set-default         Revert to previous snapshot
    delete              Delete snapshot
    list-older-than     Show snapshots older than N days
    delete-older-than   Delete snapshots older than N days
ventrical@ventrical-desktop:~$ 

```

### Post by ventrical on 2013-05-21
Actually using the 'set-default' option is a lot more efficient and direct.  The snapshots do not have to be renamed.  I am going to now  try again with a Unity session and see what happens.

Edit:

Success!! It is not a saucy bug. Unity came up  on this saucy install. I think  the solution is to choose 'set-default' option.

 Basically I can go back and forth from different stages of the particular cycles' development, and, not only that , but I can jockey between development releases and final releases.

  I think also another trick to all of this is knowing when to remove apt-btrfs-snapshot and reinstalling it, most particularily for upgrading to another release, ie; from raring to saucy.

regards

edit:

btw .. all of this has already been done with Solaris.

### Post by grahammechanical on 2013-05-23
My latest attempt to dip my toes in the btrfs water has come to a miserable end.

I created a 20GB primary partition at the front of the disk by resizing and moving two other primary partitions and they are not joking when they say, these operations could take a long time.

I then installed Raring + btrfs into what was now sda4 and this time It did not throw up any errors about not being able to install Grub into MBR sda. Then I booted into Raring + btfs and I got error sparse file not allowed -- press any key. That led briefly to the purple screen with the 5 dots and then to a black screen with a flashing cursor.

Oh well, back to the drawing board. And there I was hoping to learn about snapshots.

Sparse files?

[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sparse_file](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sparse_file)

Now, how did I get one of those? When I created the new partition I marked it as btrfs But when I installed raring did I mark it for format? Cannot remember. I guess I must reinstall and mark the partition to be formatted. I will try that.

Bug

[https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/grub2/+bug/975557](https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/grub2/+bug/975557)

Which is a duplicate of this bug

[https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/grub2/+bug/736743](https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/grub2/+bug/736743)

which says this

> [COLOR=#333333][FONT=Ubuntu Mono]In any case, this is not a new problem that arose from using subvolumes, nor does it prevent booting (you get a spurious "Press any key to continue" prompt, but if you just ignore it it'll boot anyway). Downgrading to wishlist.[/FONT][/COLOR]

It seems that I did not wait long enough. I will see.

@grahammechanical

What I do is start up with Raring or Saucy USB (or CD DVD) from boot. Then:

<Install Ubuntu>

After a few more steps Ubiquity prompts me as to what I would like to do with partitions. I choose <Something else?> *note* I think that this is where the success lies because you can format the btrfs from there and then directly install. Of course do not forget to choose '/'

  I then let the install go all the way to finish. Job done , then reboot. If I have decided in the <Something Else> option to make a new partition I make sure that it is Primary Partition. And of course -- I tic off the <Format> option.

edit: btw .. it is always good to boot back into live session and  use <disk> utility to find out your btrfs designation's  sda#  because that is important if you are not using the 'set-default' option with apt-btrfs-snapshot because  you will need to manually enter that  sda number if you move , mount and rename the file.



regards

### Post by ventrical on 2013-05-23
fyi

Quoting Colin Watson from bug report;

[INDENT]   This is actually a misleading error message: what's happening is that   GRUB's btrfs implementation doesn't implement the file read hook   interface for returning blocklists to calling code. I posted to   grub-devel about this and the upstream maintainer pointed out that,   even aside from multi-device problems, writing to btrfs from GRUB is   fundamentally risky because:
      
[LIST]
[*]the same block may be used by multiple snapshots  * every tree   which uses a given block will contain its checksum, and so on   recursively
[/LIST]
      However, btrfs reserves space at the start for the boot loader. This   space is more than GRUB needs to embed itself, and so we could use 1KB   of it for an environment block.
      In any case, this is not a new problem that arose from using   subvolumes, nor does it prevent booting (you get a spurious "Press any   key to continue" prompt, but if you just ignore it it'll boot anyway).   Downgrading to wishlist.
 [/INDENT]

---

  Using Ubiquity to install a btrfs filesystem is pretty well straight forward.  I have both a stand alone hdd with btrfs and  a hdd with both btrfs and ext4.  I did have one fail when I tried to  CREATE a partiton using <something else> then formatting it to btrfs and then trying to install on it.   The other fail I had was when I first tired to  upgrade from Precise to Raring and then rollback to Precise. I had to remove apt-btrfs-snapshot before I did the upgrade, then reinstall it after the upgrade was complete, allow me to roll back to Precise successfully.

On this system I am using a WD400AB-32BVA0 IDE hdd which is formatted  with btrfs for one system , Saucy.

 Very stable so far.

### Post by grahammechanical on 2013-05-27
If the OS is broken, requiring a reinstall and the last backup does not include a person's very latest work, then they are going to lose the latest work anyway. With a btrfs rollback they get the OS back. It all depends on a person's backup/rollback routine.

I have just tested booting into Ubuntu+btrfs (sda4) from the Grub command line. Finally got it to work by typing the Grub boot parameters for Ubuntu+btrfs from the Grub config file - a lot of typing and frustrating because of my typing errors. I do notice one strange thing not connected with this experiment.

Grub manual recommends using this set root=(hd0,msdos4) notice the brackets but the boot parameters in the Grub config show set root='hd0,msdos4'  Note the single quotation mark. The one that works is (hd0,msdos4). So, how does this Ubuntu boot correctly when booting from the Grub menu? Strange. One of the mysteries of life.

Still, I do not need to worry about a new install of Ubuntu on this hard disk not detecting the Ubuntu+btrfs.

Onward and Upward.

---

 I had two real crashes with btrfs on two IDE hdds. One a WD40GB and the other a WD80GB. The latest , on the 40GB was near catastrophic, emulating a hardware error DISK NOT FOUND error from CMOS. I swapped  the 40GB drive out to another hardware system and it will boot intermittently and then lock up or spin-down. My 500GB SATA has not reported any problems so far.

 There will be this servo polling noise coming from the hdd. That means major crash as it is trying to seek for a lost sector or write to a sector (or could be power problem). I'll have to test these drives on another system and study the SMART DATA before I blame btrfs.

  Till then .. I am approaching with caution and expecting further crashes after reading up on some of the link you have shared. It's not all a bed of roses with btrfs :)

regards

### Post by ventrical on 2013-05-27
I'll be not giving up on it, that's for sure, or until Mir comes back out for Saucy & Raring desktops. Either way it is a fun tool with a lot of potential. 

  I remember back in 1992. They told me I could not boot a MSDOS 5.0 disk without a bootsector.  There was a tool there called partn and I was able to exploit that tool, remove the 512 bytes of boot sector data and restore them to all zeros and then boot from the floppy. Of course after the boot the 512 bytes  of boot sector data was restored but I just didn't like being told  the I 'could not' do certain things. They told me I would never be able to start up Win XP on a 166MHz Pentium with only 64MBs of RAM, and I did.

  With Ubuntu and these current cycles we can use all these tools to our hearts content and modify them also.  Freedom.

### Post by grahammechanical on 2013-05-28
Are you sure that the molases effect is not due to llvmpipe? It is now the fall back video mode when a video driver is not activated (including Nouveau). I think I am correct. Ubuntu 12.04.2 might not have Unity 2D as the fall back.

Regards.

---

### Post by ventrical on 2013-05-28
Crosstalk:

 On Ultra ATA IDE drives they use 80 pin cables (40 of those are just grounds) so  to prevent a phenomenon called crosstalk. That means line noise while information is being read/wrote. I did an install of an ext4 journal file system using raring and that drive I thought was defective  is working just fine. 

  I am declaring the assumption that <the btrfs> file system is vulnerable to a phenomenon called cross talk on certain IDE, UDMA and Ultra ATA drives. This does not seem to be the case with SATA hdds as I have a successful Saucy install still running with no blips so far. This vulnerability would not necessarily apply to properly racked IDE hdds (that means installed properly with cover on).  I run a lot of my machines with cover open, thereby allowing in extra line noise. This line noise does not seem to effect ext4 JFS. I am also assuming that the btrfs format allows for more than normal syncing processes and so lock ups with an open system can be more frequent.  The documentations are apparent in the obvious results of install a stable install of raring using ext4.

  As was suggested by grahammechanical, btrfs  is probably better served by administrators who are more familiar with it's tools. Of course I'll continue to monitor the behaviour of the said hdd and make corrections to my assumptions if and   when possible.

Regards

---

### Post by ventrical on 2013-05-28
> **grahammechanical said:**
> Are you sure that the molases effect is not due to llvmpipe? It is now the fall back video mode when a video driver is not activated (including Nouveau). I think I am correct. Ubuntu 12.04.2 might not have Unity 2D as the fall back.

Regards.

Actually it was the whole boot process that took ages & yes, you are correct about llvmpipe but this is on a GeForce 210/218 which does not have llvmpipe by default (usually only after update and reboot).

### Post by ventrical on 2013-05-28
Here is basically the core of how to move the filesystem and rename it (does not have to be @_badroot .. you can name it whatever you want).  Since I can name  differently I can name one saved session @_raring and another @_ saucy , so .. when I do 
```

apt-btrfs-snapshot list

```

I can see the different names and choose the appropiate  rollback or rollup that I want to get into .. but try try to get the basic rollback first. Good luck.

**4 Rollback**

 Now let's assume  the last apt operation turned our working system  into one that isn't working as expected anymore. That's why we want to  restore the previous system state, i.e., we want to do a rollback.
 Therefore we mount the btrfs filesystem to a separate location, e.g. /mnt:
```


 mount /dev/sda1 /mnt

```



 We can now see our subvolumes in the output of:

```


 ls -l /mnt/
 root@server1:~# ls -l /mnt/
total 0
drwxr-xr-x 1 root root 230 Nov 22 10:46 @
drwxr-xr-x 1 root root 230 Nov 22 10:46 @apt-snapshot-2012-11-22_11:50:38
drwxr-xr-x 1 root root  26 Nov 22 10:57 @home
root@server1:~#

```


 @apt-snapshot-2012-11-22_11:50:38 is a snapshot of our working root filesystem (@)  before the apt operation.  In order to make the system boot from that  working snapshot instead of from the current subvolume, we rename @ to something else and then @apt-snapshot-2012-11-22_11:50:38 to @:

```


 mv /mnt/@ /mnt/@_badroot
mv /mnt/@apt-snapshot-2012-11-22_11:50:38 /mnt/@

```


 Now reboot:

```


 reboot

```

### Post by grahammechanical on 2013-05-29
Well, I am about to start a little experiment of my own. I have created the snapshot of Raring and upgraded to Saucy. I have yet to boot into it and roll back to Raring. Based upon reading a wiki page or two, reviewing Ventrical's excellent experiments and my own failures in getting a Ubuntu btrfs installation, I am going to test these things:

1) Grub & Btrfs

Leave at least 1Mib unallocated space at the beginning  of the disk as Grub needs more space than is available in the MBR when working with btrfs. Do not turn this unallocated space into a partition. This will solve the failure of grub-install to install grub into the MBR.

2) Rolling back a distribution upgrade

Use btrfs-tools to create the snapshot of the existing version and to roll back to it from a distribution upgrade to the newer version. Do not have apt-btrfs-snapshot installed. Do not use it to create the snapshot of the existing installation. During the upgrade apt-btrfs-snapshot will itself be upgraded and a newer version does not work so well with snapshots created by an older version.

3) Rolling back from a broken update

Use apt-btrfs-snapshot. It will automatically create a snapshot of the system whenever apt-get updates the system. It can be used to rollback to before the update was run. This could be very useful to users of the development branch.

Use Recovery Mode Root Shell to roll back a busted Ubuntu that cannot be booted due to an update. This will be an emulation as I have no wish to bust the OS. Remember, Recovery Mode Root Shell gives us a read only file system and we need a read/write file system.

4) Find and test a GUI application for managing the B-Tree file system.

Here goes nothing.

### Post by grahammechanical on 2013-05-29
@Ventrical

I went from Saucy back to raring and forward to Saucy by renaming subvol @ to @_badroot and @_snapshot to @ (as the tutorial says). So, it was now Raring but then I made an @_snapshot_raring and renamed @_badroot to @ and a reboot brought back Saucy again. It seems that if we have a collection of snapshots we can change to whatever one we want by renaming it to @ after first moving the sitting @ out of the way.

It might even be possible to copy a snapshot on to another partition - rename it @ and with a reboot we get an upgrade. It think that you spoke about this. I think it should be possible. Especially if the the machines have the same hardware.

I am tempted to convert a sitting Ext4 Saucy that I consider my rolling development Ubuntu over to btrfs due to the usefulness of apt-btrfs-snapshot. Imagine bringing MIR code and then reverting back and then getting MIR through a regular update. No need to purge PPA brought in experimental code. We could revert back from one monthly snapshot to the previous one.

UPDATE - UPDATE

I have just used a live session DVD to rollback a distribution upgrade to Saucy back to Raring. It is so simple. A few commands (all in the wiki) and a reboot. This is useful for those times when the OS is so broken that we cannot even get to a terminal or tty. Provided that it is a btrfs file system, for those of us just seeing this thread.

### Post by ventrical on 2013-05-31
> **ventrical said:**
> I usually choose <network> from (recovery mode) and that gives me read/write.



 OK .. that worked. Choose <network> first from recovery mode. It will then go into verbose script and loop back to the menu screnn. Then, choose  root.

Then, from root:

apt-btrfs-snapshot list

You will get the list of snapshots available.

@snapshot_name_here

Then:

```

apt-btrfs-snapshot set-default @snapshot_name_here

```

Works seamlessly.

btw .. I performed this on an hdd that has the whole disk formatted to btrfs - no other installs. Will now try that on a system with muliple installs and file formats from #root.

### Post by grahammechanical on 2013-05-31
My experiments were going so well and then, miserable failure. My priority HD is sdb with both a raring and saucy install. I have Installs of precise, quantal, & raring on sda. One raring was btrfs. I wanted to boot raring btrfs from the grub menu on sdb. Which was not detecting the btrfs raring on sda4. So, I created 1Mib of unallocated space at the front of sda and also at the front of sdb, as we are supposed to do.

But Grub did something that I did not think it could do. Unable to find vmlinuz on sda4 (btrfs) it loaded a raring (Ext4) on another partition. It jumped partitions! Unbelievable.

So, I gave boot priority to sdb and that pulled up the grub from raring-btrfs and immediately went to grub rescue. I messed around with that for a few times and decided to re-install raring into that partition. I first tried with a daily image that came out a few days before release. Then with the release image both took me to a black screen with the white flashing cursor that so many have mentioned in help sections of this forum.

So, I decided to install saucy-btrfs on sda5. I wanted to test apt-btrfs-snapshot. The first install crashed - not enough disk space. There is 10GB. The second install succeeded but also gave the black screen with the flashing white cursor. It happens just at the point that the log on screen should be loading.

Now, Nouveau has been kind to me in recent months but not this time. Recovery Resume got me to a desktop under llvmpipe and a Nvidia driver (319) seems to have fixed things. On saucy-brtfs that is.

I thought that raring was up to kernel 3.8.0-22 but installed version is 3.8.0-19. When trying to boot from the grub command line or recovery root shell we need the correct vmlinuz file. I may have been trying to boot from grub rescue a vmlinuz file that was not there.

I think that btrfs definitely has a future but to be useful to ordinary users there needs to be a foolproof GUI or a couple of carefully crafted scripts that take care of the rollbacks. I cannot see the point of offering a "revert distribution upgrade" option if it is not click and go for ordinary users.

Regards.

---

### Post by ventrical on 2013-05-31
> **grahammechanical said:**
> 

Use Recovery Mode Root Shell to roll back a busted Ubuntu that cannot be booted due to an update. This will be an emulation as I have no wish to bust the OS. Remember, Recovery Mode Root Shell gives us a read only file system and we need a read/write file system.



@grahammechanical


Here is the code to mount your system in root#  to read/write from Recovery Mode Option.

```

mount -o remount,rw /

```

---

### Post by ventrical on 2013-05-31
I have successfully completed the experiment that @grahammechanical suggested  trying to restore a snapshot from root.  The whole trick of the matter was to go to Recovery Mode Option in GRuB, Choose root# and then

```

mount -o remount,rw /

```

 and I was able to perform a seamless rollback  using
```

apt-btrfs-snapshot set-default @apt-snapshot_name_here

```
on my 500GB SATA hdd that has 6 other partitions of varying file systems.

  I did not set the 1MB partition as it appears that GRuB is handling the btrfs file system in a rock solid manner.

Regards

---

It had this  tendency to mix up the order of how the installs were laid out. Almost like a polynomial. FOIL. , but , after update-grub the first would be last, the inside would be first and the outside would be next. I am not sure why or if they have fixed that.

  I would think it is safe to say that there are some logic problems here with the loop and perhaps btrfs may have some work to be done because I have noticed anomolies that you mention.

### Post by grahammechanical on 2013-05-31
I guess from the point of view of the Grub and btrfs developers it is you and I that are the anomalities.

For the user of a single desktop the devs would expect nothing more than a single Linux install on one hard disk. And that I think works as intended. Grub does boot into a Ubuntu btrfs file system. 

And the user can roll back from an update and roll back from an upgrade to the distribution. This we have tested. The roll back can be done from a live session and it can be done from recovery mode and not just from a terminal at a working desktop.

The roll back feature will be especially useful for those running the development branch. The tricky bit comes when we want to put in more than one btrfs install of Ubuntu. Do we need more than one btrfs install in order to be a tester? Perhaps not. I set up a saucy install intending that it would be my rolling development branch through 14.04 and beyond. If life spared. I am now seriously considering re-installing saucy on that hard disk on btrfs and running snapshots at every update.

Tomorrow I am going to re-install raring on btrfs to see if I can get two btrfs installs on the same HD into the grub menu.

Regards.

### Post by ventrical on 2013-06-01
> **grahammechanical said:**

Tomorrow I am going to re-install raring on btrfs to see if I can get two btrfs installs on the same HD into the grub menu.

Regards.

The results of which should be interesting and which I will try to emulate. Somewhere along this point  I intend to attempt to  migrate one @snapshot from one install to another.  (I assume the only way this can be done is from one btrfs file system to another btrfs file system) because I had tried to move/copy a snapshot to an ext4 partition and that was a failure.

  I think  the idea here is being able to have a quick recovery during development release but it definitely would come in handy also as a system restore tool in case something wicked came in from upstream on a  stable release. The btrfs-tools (convert) does allow rollback  but only  of an image file. This means that if you convert your current ext4 to btrfs, make a snapshot and then  convert back to  ext4 you would only get a restored image and not the snapshots that you made while in the btrfs format. I'm just thinking out loud here.

---

### Post by grahammechanical on 2013-06-01
Well, I have confirmed it. Grub will show the last installed Ubuntu on a btrfs partition. It will not show an earlier install of Ubuntu on a btrfs partition on the same hard disk.

First, I booted into saucy+btrfs on sda5. Then I installed raring+btrfs on sda4 and only Ubuntu on sda4 appears in the Grub menu. I am working from it right now. I also noticed something as I was installing this raring, Ubiquity notices the btrfs partitions and gives them the type = btrfs but under system it is blank even though there is raring in one btrfs partition and saucy in another btrfs partition. Ubiquity is blank regarding the OS installed there.

I have confidence enough to have btrfs as the file system for my rolling development Ubuntu. I will use the snapshot/rollback feature with confidence. I am not so confident in Grub and Ubiquity when it comes to something more that a single btrfs install of Ubuntu. I have my doubts about Grub being able to show a second btrfs Ubuntu on another hard disk. I will test this out in a day or two.

I feel that I should point out that btrfs seems to be a file system for servers and multiply devices (hard disks) more than for the user of a single machine. I have not tested the server and logical volume aspect of btrfs. I make no comment about that side of things.

I have also notiiced that / and /home are separate subvolumes in btrfs. So, if we make a snapshot of @ ( /) and not @home (/home) then when we rollback the @ snapshot we should not also revert back to an earlier version of our documents.  Just a roll back of the system. I think.

Regards.

---

### Post by Cheesemill on 2013-06-03
I thought I'd post here instead of starting a new thread...

I've been holding off on installing Saucy until gnome-shell 3.8 hit the standard repos, but now it's hit I'm planning my installation. I'm looking to use btrfs as my filesystem but there's one thing I can't figure out. I want to enable compression on the btrfs partitions, now this is easily done after installation by adding the compress=lzo option to fstab but this only has any effect on newly created files - none of the base installation files will be compressed. I've looked in Ubiquity and the text mode installer but neither of these let you set this as a mount option during the installation process. I can think of a few possible work-arounds...


[LIST]
[*]Just install normally and then mount the btrfs partitions from a different OS and rewrite all of the files to force compression.
[*]Install the system using debootstrap so I can specify the mount options.
[*]Use a preseed file with the installation to specify mount options (not sure if this can be done, I'm just speculating).
[/LIST]
 
Does anyone have any experience/suggestions for such an installation?

PS - This shouldn't matter at all but the btrfs partitions will be created on top of a dm-crypt / LVM setup.

---

### Post by grahammechanical on 2013-06-03
Well, in for a penny - in for a pound as my granny used to say. I have installed my saucy rolling development branch as a btrfs partition. And it was only towards the end of the second attempt at installing saucy that I realised why the first attempt failed with a grub rescue prompt. I had fallen into Ubiquity's trap. I was installing on to sdb but Ubiquity was installing Grub into the MBR of sda (default location). It confirms, if it needed confirming, that Grub will not boot to an OS on a btrfs partition on another hard disk. If an install on a btrfs partition is putting Grub into the MBR of another hard disk then we do not even get a Grub menu - it goes straight to Grub rescue.

Learning all the time.

---

### Post by ventrical on 2013-06-03
> **grahammechanical said:**
>  If an install on a btrfs partition is putting Grub into the MBR of another hard disk then we do not even get a Grub menu - it goes straight to Grub rescue.

Learning all the time.

That happened here on the weekend. I failed attempt at installing saucy to a USB. Also sent my hdd to Grub Rescue Prompt (which had Windows 8 Bootloader and  Ubuntu Install) Solved with Grub Rescue CD.

 My 2 cents ... ? there is downtime in tweaking btrfs but, if you keep it simple, install to sda1 (or have an install alongside with other filesystems -just one- ) then it is a great filesystem for rolling back to almost any distro .. up or down.!!

### Post by ventrical on 2013-06-05
@grahammechanical

Running synaptic or Update Manager will call apt-btrfs-snapshot in the background.  What I do (if I do not want to take a snapshot) is:

```

sudo apt-get remove apt-btrfs-snapshot

```

then reinstall it when I need it or want to take a snapshot.

swapfile

 I guess btrfs scavenges  from the swapfile at will. How is why is beyond me because I do know that I have not been able to successfully do a btrfs install with a swapfile partition. Even on an all btrfs hdd, during initial setup,  it would not install properly , reporting errors  etc..

---

### Post by grahammechanical on 2013-06-05
@ventrical

Thanks. Remove, seems a bit drastic. Thinking of it from btrfs being the default Ubuntu file system point of view.

Did you notice that Jorge picked up on us experimenting with btrfs when I made that post on the new style (just playing around) forum? I am thinking of starting a thread on that new style forum. I am writing up some advice on using btrfs. I call it adventures in btrfs. Would you join with me? In a day or two I will post what I have come up with. Would you post any corrections or additions? Then I will edit the first post. I do not want it to be a tutorial. More like advice. What we are finding might get noticed further up the food chain. It might move things forward a bit.

Because of having Ubuntu already installed, the swap file already exists. I have not tried installing btrfs using the whole disk. I have not had that swap file issue. I do think that our work is useful, especially if people are thinking of one day making it Ubuntu's default file system and we can get them to see our results.

### Post by ventrical on 2013-06-05
A Hardware Note:

  I just wanted to make a hardware note here since I am using Saucy and btrfs.

 I have had two WD IDE  (one IDE and one EIDE) hdds acting up and ready to scavenge them for magnets. Just now , during a hard startup, I notice a clicking on my drive.  I took off the 4 pin power cable and lo and behold, the +5v female pin was wore open to a greater diameter that the male pin. It is a newer, off market power supply (cheap) and the connectors are made from a cheaper tin alloy. I solved it temporarily by using a tool to close the connector.

 Lesson of the day .. 'check your hardware from time to time'. Especially power supplies.  7 times out of 10 , most problems arise from faulty power or not enough power, often resulting in what could appear to be  Ubuntru upgrade errors.

### Post by ventrical on 2013-06-28
> **EgoGratis said:**
> You are probably talking about entering grub 2 recovery menu and having an option to restore btrfs snapshot from that menu instead of dropping into and using shell prompt? If that is the case then great indeed.

Yes .. (recovery mode) in GRuB. I was already there. All one has to do is (assuming that you have installed the <apt-btrfs-snapshot> program)  choose recovery mode from GRub.

Choose #root option.

Enter:

```

mount -o remount,rw /

```

then:

exit[IMG]http://ubuntuforums.org/;base64,[/IMG]

...wait .. and the new screen will come up with the apt-snapshot option.

Edit:

I tried to upload a .jpg file. It was there , then gone .. now the editor is acting funny here. Also there is a funny relic on the screen. I'll put that screen shot on the other forum.

### Post by grahammechanical on 2013-06-28
> [COLOR=#000000]How much additional space does one snapshot take?[/COLOR]

Not an easy answer to give. I have just been trying to find out. It is all transparent to the user. Where are these snapshots kept? To work with snapshots we mount the partition to /mnt. Nautilus>properties give these results

/mnt = 286 bytes
@ = / in btrfs size = 7.1GB
snapshot taken 04-06-2013 = 3.6GB size
snapshot taken 28-06-2013 = 7.1GB
Daily updates and daily snapshots = 92 snapshots since 04-06-2013. It gives 4 duplicate snapshots. That is why there are so many. There is a delete command that lets us delete snapshots older than n number of days. I have just deleted snapshots older than 2 days. That leaves 12 snapshots.

Disk Usage Analyser said disks usage = 21.8GB Now it says disk usage = 16GB. So, those 80 deleted snapshots = 5.8GB. Something is not adding up. And it is a good thing too.

Need a good routine when using snapshots on the development branch. Documentation (which is often over my head) speaks of only keeping a record of the changes and not simply creating a file system copy. It is automagical.

Regards.

Edit: @ventrical

You are right the snapshot rollback is in Recovery menu. I just needed to run update-grub to get it there. That will be useful.

---

There are already a few of the forum members that post  regularly in this sub-forum, that have a presence on Ubuntu-Discourse

[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Discourse_%28software%29](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Discourse_%28software%29)

Trying to reinvent internet forum software.

> Documentation (which is often over my head) speaks of only keeping a  record of the changes and not simply creating a file system copy. It is  automagical.

Regards.

Yes it doesn't just duplicate the amount of data. Thanks for the numbers and now i will have to test it myself when i will do new dev release install. Tutorial will help to avoid known issues and thanks for that again!

---

### Post by grahammechanical on 2013-06-30
The option to revert back a snapshot does not appear in the standard recovery menu until we run the grub or network options. After doing their stuff we get back to the recovery menu and there is an apt-snapshot option. Some of the other menu options might do the trick as well. Not tested those. A post from ventrical says it can be done by selecting the root option and running

```
mount -o remount,rw /
```
```
exit
```

So, we need the file system in read/write mode to get the apt-snapshot option.

Regards.

---

### Post by ventrical on 2013-07-09
I was doing a thougth experiement with mir and decided to do a roll-back to when I first built this saucy+btrfs install. Although the mir ppa was still there (according to synaptic) , none of the files were installed. Also, it wanted to update the 3.10.0.2  kernel . The good news story is that I could not get new upgrades because of two programs that would break the new xserver and the roll back fixed that (it appears). I did not yet actually do the upgrade after the rollback. 

  The other good news is that all my bookmarks are still there.  This little btrfs program has been  a bright spot in this development release. It is almost like an unbreakable kernel but I won't go that far just yet.

edit:

Actually the  3.10.0.2 kernel is still in Grub. Fascinating. So I am to go ahead with update (or re-update) and see what happens then.

FAZoooom.... :)

edit:

actually Fazoommm nothing. I still have those two held packages.

edit:
I opened synaptic and unticked the mir ppa, then:

reload, mark and now all new updates are being applied. No held packages.

### Post by grahammechanical on 2013-07-11
@ventrical

I would like to make use of your extensive experience in testing btrfs. I have a broken saucy+btrfs install. I am tempted to reinstall but it does not have a separate /home partition and as we have found out Ubiquity crashes when doing a re-install on btrfs without a reformat as well. It fails to read the user details. So,

I am thinking, a normal snapshot covers everything under @ and that includes @home. But what if I took a snapshot of @home and copied off the partition and then reinstalled + format btrfs to the partition and then copied the @home snapshot back in? Might that get me a working saucy in the image of the present one?

Oh, I am still working on developing recovery mode apt-snapshot as a snapshot manager or reverter at least. Although the scripts are shell scripts they are not running from the command line. They are executable and I am using sudo and from the directory the scripts are in. I think that they need to be renamed .sh. Not yet tested that.

Regards.

---

### Post by ventrical on 2013-07-11
> **grahammechanical said:**
> @ventrical

I would like to make use of your extensive experience in testing btrfs. I have a broken saucy+btrfs install. I am tempted to reinstall but it does not have a separate /home partition and as we have found out Ubiquity crashes when doing a re-install on btrfs without a reformat as well. It fails to read the user details. So,
Regards,

   I'll have to attempt to answer this in two parts. I'll address the above at present and your following thought experiment later. 

   I have had several  broken installs on btrfs.  I would get  things like no Unity panel, black space or  persistent kernel . The worst of these is Grub Rescue> prompt

Method 1

  I had restored several disabled btrfs desktops with Grub Rescue (not super Grub Rescue) using the *live* CD. The trick (in these instances of breakage) is that you want to restore Grub (not install it). I can safely say that there is an 80% chance of "success"  Using Grub Rescue CD in making a broken btrfs install fully bootable. If you decide to use the *live* CD you just want to log on live , choose  (restore grub). It will pop up with different windows at which you hit <OK>  After a time you will get a "success" notification that Grub (and the partion in question) were restored. THEN, reboot and your btrfs partition is there and bootable in the Grub menu.

Method 2

 This one is tricky as there were a few occasions where I lost Unity desktop. I could get terminal on the desktop with <ctrl+alt+t> and also <change desktop background>  with right click, also bringing in the option to run the settings program.  The first part of this method will not work. What we want to do is keep the install rolling rather than to try and do a fresh install. So , what I do is try to add a new user with permissions. If you use the first part of the above method it will fail because it will not accept your password. So we Do this next part. If you can get to  terminal from Grub Recovery mode , choose #root and make sure to:

mount -o remount,rw /

AND THEN  

sudo apt-get install gnome-session-fallback
*caution* If you can get this far then you may want to disable (remove) apt-btrfs-snapshot first before you install gnome-session-fallback because there may be a problem with the number of snapshots or some other bug.

sudo apt-get remove apt-btrfs-snapshot

If your lightdm is working then you want to log on to Gnome-Session-fallback (I forget the new name for it right off hand - Flashback ? ) and then create a new user there  with permissions. THEN you can log in to Unity with full panel, harvest all the other files from the other user_account and  other settings/bookmarks as well.

 I am assuming that none of the above applies to the scenario which you speak and so I will have to review your suggested thought experiment and see if there is anything I can add.

Regards..

---

On a singlular drive this has not been my experience. I recall we both experimented with btrfs-tools convert package that converted an ext4 install of saucy to btrfs while creating a 'chunk' file that can be rolled back later. In my test I used a WD 40GB IDE drive. The original OS was Lucid Lynx.  I stored some files, bookmarks  and etc .. and then converted it to btrfs. THEN I took a saucy-desktop-i386.iso USB and installed it . Ubiquity asked me if I wanted to *upgrade*, yes, and it installed it  (without format) pulling in not only home files , but , also the 'chunk' file that was created with btrfs-tools convert.

 Now I am going where angels fear to tread .. :) I can only assume that if you choose to do a re-install and choose the upgrade option that it will install with btrfs, however, if that option is not offered during the Ubiquity loop then it is obvious that the partition is not being detected
  and also I assume you are usng a partition on a  drive with other installs (not a singular drive) so I would not try this.

 I am still reviewing your thought experiment :)

 I re-studied the native btrfs commands and   it looks like a lot of cli code entry at this point but I can see now how to move one  @snapshot from one partition to another - still very dicey stuff.

```

 btrfs send [-v] [-i <subvol>] [-p <parent>] <subvol>
        Send the subvolume to stdout.

        Sends the subvolume specified by <subvol> to stdout.
        By default, this will send the whole subvolume. To do
        an incremental send, one or multiple '-i <clone_source>'
        arguments have to be specified. A 'clone source' is
        a subvolume that is known to exist on the receiving
        side in exactly the same state as on the sending side.

        Normally, a good snapshot parent is searched automatically
        in the list of 'clone sources'. To override this, use
        '-p <parent>' to manually specify a snapshot parent.
        A manually specified snapshot parent is also regarded
        as 'clone source'.

 -v               Enable verbose debug output. Each
                         occurrency of this option increases the
                         verbose level more.
        -i <subvol>      Informs btrfs send that this subvolume,
                         can be taken as 'clone source'. This can
                         be used for incremental sends.
        -p <subvol>      Disable automatic snaphot parent
                         determination and use <subvol> as parent.
                         This subvolume is also added to the list
                         of 'clone sources' (see -i).
        -f <outfile>     Output is normally written to stdout.
                         To write to a file, use this option.
                         An alternative would be to use pipes.



```

```

 btrfs receive [-v] [-i <infile>] <mount>
        Receive subvolumes from stdin.

        Receives one or more subvolumes that were previously 
        sent with btrfs send. The received subvolumes are stored
        into <mount>.
        btrfs receive will fail in case a receiving subvolume
        already exists. It will also fail in case a previously
        received subvolume was changed after it was received.
        After receiving a subvolume, it is immediately set to
        read only.

        -v               Enable verbose debug output. Each
                         occurrency of this option increases the
                         verbose level more.
        -f <infile>      By default, btrfs receive uses stdin
                         to receive the subvolumes. Use this
                         option to specify a file to use instead.


```

 and this command shows me the info and path of my harddrive from my current running USB install.

```

root@ventrical-Transcendv3:~# btrfs filesystem show
Label: none  uuid: 20f80191-4ba1-4369-95ac-62a67bff23c5
    Total devices 1 FS bytes used 19.18GB
    devid    1 size 50.79GB used 24.04GB path /dev/sdb12


```

 where sdb12 is a btrfs partiton on my 500GB SATA. So basically .. it appears the tools are here to be able to possibly *import* a snapshot from another partition and then subsequently rollback to that snapshot!

 if this is the case then even a snapshot from a seemingly busted and impossible install can be imported to a new partiton.  I have already validated that the snapshot are available from a non working btrfs partition. It just looks like  one would have to be careful a bit. Of course I'll research this code and then try to test it.

  Umm .. i am assuming that the btrfs sub-system is a system in and of itself and can only be handled by  specific tools appropiated to it and also  the above size in GBs is not an accurate representation as reported by other system tools.

Edit:

Reading up on btrfs ... a lot of info. A lot of users gone before us and a lot of bugs and uncertain patches for send/recieve option. I still think there is an easier way .  If I can find it ?

### Post by grahammechanical on 2013-07-11
There are a lot of built-in btrfs commands. Most of them useful for system admins. This is what I have learnt to do without using apt-btrfs-snapshot

1) sudo mount /dev/sda# /mnt

Things do not work unless that is done.

2) To create a snapshot: sudo btrfs subvolume snapshot /mnt/@ /mnt/@_snapshot_name_

It essentially is like copying and renaming the @ (root) folder.

3a) To revert: sudo mv /mnt/@ /mnt/@_badroot

Again that is like renaming the @ (root) folder. We now have no root folder.

3b) sudo mv /mnt/@_snapshot_name /mnt/@

That is like renaming the copy of @ (root) folder back to @. Now we have a root folder again.

If we run ls -1 /mnt/ we get a list of btrfs folders including snapshots. Example:

> @
@apt-snapshot-2013-07-07_19:55:23
@apt-snapshot-2013-07-07_19:55:25
@apt-snapshot-2013-07-07_19:55:26
@apt-snapshot-2013-07-07_19:55:27
@apt-snapshot-2013-07-10_18:23:37
@apt-snapshot-2013-07-10_18:23:40
@apt-snapshot-old-root-2013-07-10_18:36:19
@apt-snapshot-old-root-2013-07-10_18:40:35
@home
@_snapshot_20130707
@_snapshot_20130707abs
@_snapshot_20130707mir

Notice, we have @ and @home = / & /home. I am taking snapshots of @ which will include @home. I am going to try taking a snapshot of @home, copying it to another partition, fresh installing and then copying the snapshot back. I have thought through my plan. I will try it tomoorow. 

It could be that the 'home folder is the ket to cloning Ubuntu_btrfs installs. Have just a copy of @home on the USB stick that installs Ubuntu and simple to copy it on to the new install and over write the newly created @home and we may have a standardized user set up.

Oh, the standard Ubuntu tools do give measure partitions accurately. The Details page has doubled the size of this partition. And Nautilus will measure a snapshot as if it was a copy of the folder when in actual fact it is just a few KB in size.

Regards.

### Post by ventrical on 2013-07-11
I spent 4 hours on a proceedure involving this .. it was going just nice .. and then ... for some reason (momentary lapse of reason) I  sudo update-grub (ed) and now I have borked both installs on both devices, (new uuid numbers so Grub rescue CD will not recover those installs).. but I can write down the uuid numbers and mount them. 

  Thanks for sharing  what you have learned. It gets better I think :) I'm pooped .. I'll pick it up again tommorrow..

edit:

This was not at all as bad as it seemed. When I did the 'sudo update-grub' I had two seperate btrfs devices. One, a USB flash (which was the running btrfs+saucy OS)and secondly a 40GB hdd running btrfs +saucy. Grub somehow switched the UUID numbers. Also, with the hdd install and a 'live' saucy USB also installed the hdd over-rode the USB priority boot! That means I had to use the Plop Boot manager to boot the 'live' saucy USB. Somewhere in this btrfs+grub process , grub assigned the hdd as a CDROM drive . I think it is both a harware logic error and a Grub bug because this has happened on two different machines now.

  I had to unmount the physical hdd (disconnect the cable) to discover that my USB flash install fo saucy was able to boot up. I will try to fix the hdd by editing in the correct UUID or trying Grub Rescue without the USB flash installed.

I know it seems I am a little off topic here. I'm just thinking out loud a 2:30 am :)

### Post by grahammechanical on 2013-07-12
> [COLOR=#000000]I am going to try taking a snapshot of @home, copying it to another partition, fresh installing and then copying the snapshot back.[/COLOR]

It failed. The practical stuff worked but when I rebooted it said an error has occurred while mount /home S for Skip mount M for Manual repair = further info that @home was not a valid subvolume. 

I have rechecked my procedures but I cannot see how they were incomplete. I think I would have been more successful if I had simply copied the /home ('home) folder somewhere and then copied it back rather than making a snapshot of it.

Snapshots are not a complete record of the folder but of the changes to the folder.

Regards.

 I got this far.

```

root@ventrical-Transcendv3:~# mount /dev/sda2 /mnt
root@ventrical-Transcendv3:~# mv /mnt/@_badroot /mnt/@
root@ventrical-Transcendv3:~# 


```

What happened here is that I was doing an experiement (in light of some of your suggestions) and I was able to mount a btrfs filesystem while  running a  USB flash with saucy+btrfs. Please note that the USB was an actual instal of saucy on the USB (32GB ver3.0) not a live or persistive drive. After I mouted the btrfs partition on my 1.5TB hdd I then proceeded to.

```

mv /mnt/@ /mnt/@_badroot

```

and then 

```

mv /mnt/@apt-snapshot-2012-11-22_11:50:38 /mnt/@

```

however I put in an accidental // and the result was that I got Grub Rescue on My SATA boot and could not recover with the Grub Rescue CD

 Ok . having said all that I booted again from my USB drive and then clicked the 250GB partion (in the Unity side panel) and I could see all the snapshots. I also noticed that:

@

was now 

@_badroot

!!

Interesting I thought. Then I proceeded to see if I could reverse this process..


mount /dev/sda2 /mnt
mv /mnt/@_badroot /mnt/@

and right before my eyes I could see the  @_badroot file change back to @ in nautilus.

Now I haven't rebooted and  I am tempted to  sudo update-grub  : but I will not do that now.

I am going to reboot and see whats up on the hdd and see if I can recover with Grub Rescue.

 Anyways .. your theory is correct. These files can be manipulated on other btrfs installs with those built in btrfs commands across different hardware devices.

Well graham.. I am just baffled.  That proceedure allowed me to boot right into btrfs (did not have to use Grub Rescue disk .. etc..)

I never thought it would be this simple. This then means that you can move, mount and  rename any @apt-snapshot on another device and rename a good, working snapshot to  @ which will be  a bootable restore on a device or install that is busted.  This is much more streamlined (don't need Ubiquity).

  My assumption then is that when an apt-snapshot is mv, mounted and renamed (as in the directions of my previous post) that snapshot will become root '@'  then the GRub portion is also restored and also that the snapshot process is capturing the Grub Boot menu also.

Regards..
